claim.hypotheses_location: Page 1020, introduction: paragraph beginning “We hypothesized that mortality salience should produce divergent cultural responses.”

data.source: Laboratory experiments with participant samples completing writing primes and subsequent attitude/attribution measures
data.sample_size: Study 1: 42 participants (22 European Americans, 20 Asian Americans). Study 2: 71 participants (48 European Americans, 23 Asian Americans).
data.notes: Study 1 measures: Prostitution Attitudes Scale (PAS; 5 items, Likert −3 to +3, α = .74) and bail amount (guidance: $0–$999). Study 2 measure: Assignment of Blame Scale (ABS; 4 items, 1–7, α = .62). Condition manipulation: mortality-salience (own death) vs. control (dental pain) writing task; PANAS used as delay task. Asian American samples included Chinese, Japanese, or Korean descent; Study 1 had 10 first-generation and 10 second-generation; Study 2 had 4 first-generation and 19 second-generation. Mood (net PANAS) and gender were examined and showed no effects.

method.description: Two experiments tested culturally divergent responses to mortality salience among European Americans and Asian Americans. Participants were randomly assigned to write about their own death (mortality salience) or dental pain (control), completed PANAS as a delay, and then evaluated either a culturally nonnormative target (a prostitute; Study 1) or an innocent victim (Study 2). Outcomes were analyzed with 2×2 ANOVAs/MANOVAs and planned contrasts to assess condition by ethnicity effects.
steps: Recruit European American and Asian American participants.
Randomly assign participants to mortality-salience (write about own death) or control (write about dental pain) condition.
Administer PANAS as a delay task.
Study 1: Present fabricated legal brief about a prostitute awaiting bail; collect bail amount decision and administer the Prostitution Attitudes Scale (PAS).
Study 2: Present fabricated news article about an innocent victim (“Steve”) in a car accident; administer the Assignment of Blame Scale (ABS).
Compute composite scores (PAS, ABS) and summarize bail amounts.
Analyze effects using 2 (condition) × 2 (ethnicity) MANOVA/ANOVAs and planned t-test contrasts within ethnic groups.
Check for mood (net PANAS) and gender effects; report none.
Interpret effects as culturally divergent responses to mortality salience.
method.models: 2×2 multivariate ANOVA (MANOVA) and univariate ANOVAs; planned independent-samples and within-cell t-test contrasts
method.outcome_variable: Study 1: Prostitution Attitudes Scale (PAS) score and bail amount; Study 2: Assignment of Blame Scale (ABS) score
method.independent_variables: Condition (mortality salience vs. control), Ethnicity (European American vs. Asian American)
method.control_variables: None explicitly controlled; gender and mood (net PANAS) tested and found non-significant

results.summary: Mortality salience produced culturally divergent responses. Study 1: European Americans showed more negative attitudes toward a nonnormative other (lower PAS; marginally higher bail) under mortality salience, whereas Asian Americans showed more favorable attitudes (higher PAS; nonsignificant trend to lower bail). Study 2: European Americans assigned more blame to an innocent victim under mortality salience, whereas Asian Americans assigned less blame. Gender and mood did not account for the effects. Despite divergent processes, mortality salience led to convergence in outcomes across cultures (groups differed in control but not under mortality salience).

metadata.original_paper_title: Culturally Divergent Responses to Mortality Salience
